review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

slides.title: Learning from Leaders
slides.lead: Meet four brave leaders and discover the good values they can teach us.

media.alt: Four coloured figures standing together under a bright sun.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='130' width='320' height='50' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='270' cy='42' r='24' fill='#e8a13a'/><g><circle cx='70' cy='78' r='14' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='60' y='94' width='20' height='40' rx='4' fill='#8a1f1f'/><circle cx='120' cy='72' r='14' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='110' y='88' width='20' height='46' rx='4' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='170' cy='78' r='14' fill='#3f6b9b'/><rect x='160' y='94' width='20' height='40' rx='4' fill='#3f6b9b'/><circle cx='220' cy='72' r='14' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='210' y='88' width='20' height='46' rx='4' fill='#7a5a3a'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Welcome the class warmly. Tell learners that today they will meet four real leaders from history. Explain that we do not just learn what leaders did - we learn the good values they showed, like fairness, courage and kindness. Ask: who is a leader you know? A teacher, a coach, a parent?

slides.title: What is a leader?
slides.lead: A leader is someone who helps other people and shows them a good way to go.

blocks.text: A **leader** is a person other people choose to follow. Good leaders are not just bosses. They care about other people and try to do what is **fair** and **right**.

items: Good leaders listen to others and treat everyone the same.
They are brave and stand up for what is right, even when it is hard.
We can learn good **values** from them, like honesty, kindness and hope.

slides.notes: Keep language concrete for Grade 4. Explain the word 'values' simply: values are the good things we believe in and try to do. Write a few value words on the board (fairness, courage, kindness, hope). Learners will spot these values in each leader today.

slides.title: Nelson Mandela
slides.lead: A boy from the countryside who grew up to lead all of South Africa.

blocks.text: **Nelson Mandela** was born in 1918 in a small village in the Eastern Cape. His family called him *Rolihlahla*. As a man he worked to make South Africa fair for **all** people, no matter their skin colour.

items: Long ago, South Africa had unfair laws that treated people differently by colour.
Mandela stood up against these unfair laws.
He was put in prison for 27 years, but he never gave up hope.

media.alt: A calm portrait shape of a smiling man with grey hair.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 180' role='img'><rect width='180' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='90' cy='96' r='60' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='90' cy='82' r='34' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M56 78 a34 34 0 0 1 68 0 q-34 -22 -68 0 Z' fill='#e6e1d8'/><path d='M62 118 q28 26 56 0 v40 h-56 Z' fill='#3f6b9b'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain apartheid gently and simply for young learners: long ago, unfair laws separated people by the colour of their skin and were not fair. Do not go into heavy detail. Focus on Mandela's determination and hope. The 27 years in prison shows how much he cared.

slides.title: Forgiveness and fairness
slides.lead: When Mandela came out of prison, he chose peace instead of anger.

blocks.text: It always seems impossible until it is done.
In 1994 South Africa held its first election where **everyone** could vote. Mandela became our first democratically elected president. He asked people to **forgive** and work together.

items: Value: **forgiveness** - letting go of anger to build peace.
Value: **fairness** - everyone should be treated equally.
Value: **hope** - believing things can get better.

slides.notes: This is the heart of the Mandela story for Grade 4: he could have been angry after prison, but he chose forgiveness and peace. Ask learners: when have you forgiven a friend? How did it feel? Connect the value to their own lives.

slides.title: Mahatma Gandhi
slides.lead: A leader who showed that you can stand up for what is right without fighting.

blocks.text: **Mohandas Gandhi** came to South Africa from India in 1893 to work as a lawyer. Here he was treated unfairly because he was Indian. This made him decide to fight unfair rules - but in a **peaceful** way.

items: Gandhi taught people to protest **without violence**. He called this *satyagraha*, which means 'the power of truth'.
People followed his example and refused to obey unfair laws.
Later he returned to India and helped lead his country to freedom. Many call him *Mahatma*, meaning 'great soul'.

media.alt: A simple figure with round glasses and a walking stick.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 180' role='img'><rect width='180' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><circle cx='90' cy='96' r='60' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='90' cy='78' r='30' fill='#c9a37a'/><circle cx='80' cy='76' r='7' fill='none' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='100' cy='76' r='7' fill='none' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='87' y1='76' x2='93' y2='76' stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M64 112 q26 22 52 0 v44 h-52 Z' fill='#f0ece3'/><line x1='128' y1='96' x2='128' y2='156' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain satyagraha simply as 'the power of truth' or 'peaceful protest'. The key idea for Grade 4: Gandhi showed you can be strong and brave without hurting anyone. Note that Gandhi's peaceful ideas later inspired Mandela and other leaders around the world.

slides.title: Truth and peace
slides.lead: Gandhi believed the strongest thing you can do is stay peaceful and honest.

blocks.heading: Truth
blocks.text: Always be honest, even when it is difficult.

blocks.heading: Courage
blocks.text: Be brave enough to stand up for what is right.

slides.notes: Use the three cards as talking points. Ask learners for classroom examples: how can we solve an argument peacefully at school? How can we tell the truth even when we are scared? Keep it practical and close to their world.

slides.title: Chief Albert Luthuli
slides.lead: A teacher and chief who led with calm and peace.

blocks.text: **Albert Luthuli** was a school teacher and a Zulu chief. He became the leader of the African National Congress (ANC), a group that worked for fair rights for all South Africans. Like Gandhi, he believed in **peaceful** protest.

items: In 1960 he won the **Nobel Peace Prize** - a very famous award for people who work for peace.
He was the first African person ever to win this prize.
He taught people to be patient, calm and brave.

media.alt: A gold peace prize medal with a ribbon.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 180 180' role='img'><rect width='180' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M76 40 l-18 44 h20 l12 -40 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M104 40 l18 44 h-20 l-12 -40 Z' fill='#3f6b9b'/><circle cx='90' cy='112' r='40' fill='#e8a13a'/><circle cx='90' cy='112' r='30' fill='#f0c467'/><path d='M90 92 l6 18 h19 l-15 11 6 18 -16 -11 -16 11 6 -18 -15 -11 h19 Z' fill='#b9821f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Focus on Luthuli as a peaceful leader who was honoured worldwide. Explain the Nobel Peace Prize simply: a special award for people who help bring peace. Being the first African to win it in 1960 shows the world respected his peaceful way. Link his values back to Gandhi's peaceful protest.

slides.title: Walter Sisulu
slides.lead: A wise and loyal leader who guided and supported others.

blocks.text: **Walter Sisulu** was a close friend and helper of Nelson Mandela. He was known for being **wise** and for giving good advice. He worked hard so that all South Africans could be treated fairly.

items: He helped young leaders, including Mandela, to grow and lead.
Like Mandela, he was sent to prison for standing up against unfair laws.
He never stopped believing in a fair and free South Africa.

media.alt: Two figures standing side by side, one with a hand on the other's shoulder.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 180' role='img'><rect width='200' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='140' width='200' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='70' cy='60' r='20' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='54' y='82' width='32' height='58' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><circle cx='130' cy='60' r='20' fill='#7a5a3a'/><rect x='114' y='82' width='32' height='58' rx='6' fill='#3f6b9b'/><rect x='84' y='90' width='34' height='9' rx='4' fill='#2f5233'/></svg>

slides.notes: Sisulu teaches the value of loyalty, wisdom and supporting others - a leader does not have to be the most famous person to be important. Explain that Sisulu helped and mentored Mandela. Ask learners: who helps and supports you? How can they help and support a friend?

slides.title: The values our leaders share
slides.lead: Four different leaders, but many of the same good values.

blocks.heading: Fairness
blocks.text: Mandela wanted everyone to be treated equally.

blocks.heading: Peace
blocks.text: Gandhi and Luthuli chose peace, not violence.

blocks.heading: Courage
blocks.text: All four were brave, even when it was dangerous.

blocks.heading: Kindness
blocks.text: Sisulu supported and helped the people around him.

slides.notes: This slide ties the four leaders together. Point out that even though they lived in different times and places, they shared values. Ask learners which value they think is the most important and why. There is no wrong answer - encourage discussion.

slides.title: A journey through time

events.label: Luthuli wins the Nobel Peace Prize
Mandela becomes president; everyone can vote

slides.notes: Help learners read a simple timeline from left to right. Point out that these leaders lived at different times, and some of their lives overlapped. Note that the years are not in a straight order here on purpose - you can ask learners to help put them in the right order as a quick activity.

slides.title: Being a leader in your own life
slides.lead: You do not have to be famous to be a good leader.

blocks.text: You can show the same good values as these leaders **every day** at home, at school and with your friends.

items: Be **fair** - share and give everyone a turn.
Be **kind** - help someone who is struggling.
Be **brave** - stand up for a friend who is being treated badly.
Be **honest** - always tell the truth.

media.alt: A child figure helping another child up, with a heart above them.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='124' width='220' height='36' fill='#c8b89a'/><circle cx='80' cy='60' r='16' fill='#3f6b9b'/><rect x='68' y='78' width='24' height='46' rx='6' fill='#3f6b9b'/><circle cx='140' cy='72' r='16' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='128' y='90' width='24' height='34' rx='6' fill='#2f5233'/><rect x='92' y='92' width='36' height='9' rx='4' fill='#3f6b9b'/><path d='M110 24 a9 9 0 0 1 16 0 a9 9 0 0 1 16 0 q0 12 -16 22 q-16 -10 -16 -22 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Bring the lesson home to the learners' own lives. This is the CAPS aim: learning values from leaders and applying them. Ask each learner to name one thing they will do this week to show a good value. You could turn this into a class 'values wall'.

slides.eyebrow: Let us check what we learned
slides.title: Review and remember
slides.lead: Answer these questions to show what you have learned about our leaders.

blocks.text: Today we met four brave leaders - **Nelson Mandela**, **Mahatma Gandhi**, **Chief Albert Luthuli** and **Walter Sisulu** - and the good values they teach us.

blocks.question: Name two leaders you learned about today and one value each one teaches us.
Gandhi and Luthuli both believed in one special way of standing up for what is right. What was it?
Write about one value from this lesson and how you can show it at school this week.

slides.notes: Use these three questions as a written activity or a class discussion. Question 1 checks recall, question 2 checks understanding (peaceful protest / non-violence), and question 3 asks learners to apply a value to their own life, which matches the CAPS focus. Praise effort and remind learners that anyone can be a good leader by living good values.
